report_metadata.cancer_type: Cervical Node Squamous Cell Carcinoma — Unknown Primary, HPV-Negative (T0N2bM0)
report_metadata.stage: T0N2bM0 (AJCC 8th Edition Stage III — HPV-negative unknown primary)

treatments.name: Neck Dissection Followed by Adjuvant Cisplatin-Based Chemoradiation with Comprehensive Mucosal Irradiation

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO 2020 guideline and NCCN-recommended approach for HPV-negative SCCUP with N2b disease; supported by multiple retrospective cohort studies and institutional series with consistent outcomes data

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS of 67.7-71% for HPV-negative CUP with neck dissection plus adjuvant chemoradiation; 5-year DSS of 82-86%; significantly superior to radiation alone (HR 4.45 for adjuvant RT alone vs CRT)

accessibility.rationale: Standard of care approach available at all major cancer centers; cisplatin and radiation widely available; neck dissection is standard surgical oncology procedure

safety_profile.rationale: Comprehensive mucosal irradiation (bilateral neck, pharynx, larynx) carries significant toxicity: grade 3/4 mucositis 33%, dermatitis 41%, dysphagia 15%; high-dose cisplatin associated with nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity; only 68% complete all chemotherapy cycles; PEG tube often required

biomarker_match.rationale: Standard approach for HPV-negative CUP regardless of specific biomarkers; comprehensive mucosal fields specifically indicated for HPV-negative unknown primary where primary site cannot be identified; TP53 mutation may reduce cisplatin sensitivity but CRT remains standard

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Multimodal approach: surgical resection removes gross nodal disease providing pathologic staging and local control; cisplatin acts as a radiosensitizer via DNA cross-linking and inhibition of DNA repair; comprehensive mucosal irradiation (70 Gy to neck, 54-60 Gy elective to bilateral pharyngeal and laryngeal mucosa) targets potential occult primary and residual microscopic disease across all possible mucosal primary sites

key_evidence.study_name: Multicenter Retrospective Analysis of HNCUP Outcomes (Muller von der Grun et al.)

biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker required for eligibility

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab plus Cisplatin/5-FU (KEYNOTE-048 Regimen) for Recurrent/Metastatic Setting

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (KEYNOTE-048) with 882 patients demonstrated significant OS benefit; FDA-approved first-line treatment for R/M HNSCC; 5-year follow-up data now available

survival_benefit.rationale: For PD-L1 CPS >=1: median OS 13.6 months vs 10.6 months (HR 0.64); for total population: OS 13.0 vs 10.7 months (HR 0.71); ORR 37.2% in CPS >=1; applicable if disease recurs or is deemed unresectable

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved first-line regimen for R/M HNSCC; pembrolizumab widely available; this patient with PD-L1 CPS 5 qualifies for both pembrolizumab monotherapy and combination under CPS >=1 indication

safety_profile.rationale: Combination adds immune-related adverse events (thyroid dysfunction, pneumonitis, hepatitis) to chemotherapy toxicities; grade 3-5 treatment-related AEs in ~70% with combination; manageable with appropriate monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 CPS 5 meets the CPS >=1 threshold for pembrolizumab approval; combination regimen shows benefit across CPS subgroups; relevant as salvage or if primary presents as unresectable recurrent/metastatic disease

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint, restoring T-cell-mediated antitumor immunity. Combined with cisplatin (DNA cross-linking) and 5-fluorouracil (thymidylate synthase inhibition), the regimen provides both immune activation and direct cytotoxic effects against HNSCC cells
biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 CPS >=1 (this patient has CPS 5 — qualifies)

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy (KEYNOTE-048 Single Agent)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (KEYNOTE-048) validated; FDA-approved for PD-L1 CPS >=1 R/M HNSCC; robust long-term follow-up data available

survival_benefit.rationale: For CPS >=1: median OS 12.3 vs 10.4 months (HR 0.74); however ORR only 19.1% for CPS >=1; CPS 1-19 subgroup shows weaker PFS benefit than CPS >=20; primarily relevant for R/M setting

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved; widely available; simpler regimen than combination; may be preferred for patients unable to tolerate platinum chemotherapy

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than combination regimen; lower rate of grade 3-5 AEs (~17% vs ~70% with combo); immune-related AEs generally manageable; avoids cisplatin toxicity

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 CPS 5 qualifies under CPS >=1 indication; however benefit is more modest in CPS 1-19 range compared to CPS >=20; TP53 mutation status does not preclude response

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the interaction between PD-1 on T cells and PD-L1/PD-L2 on tumor cells, reinvigorating exhausted tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and restoring antitumor immune surveillance. Efficacy depends on pre-existing tumor-reactive T-cell populations

treatments.name: Definitive Cisplatin-Based Concurrent Chemoradiation with Comprehensive Mucosal Fields (No Upfront Surgery)

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN-recommended alternative for patients who are not surgical candidates or prefer organ preservation; multiple institutional series show 2-year OS of 89% and LRC of 89% with definitive CRT

survival_benefit.rationale: 2-year OS 89%, 2-year LRC 89%, 2-year PFS 79% in definitive CRT series; however upfront neck dissection provides superior locoregional recurrence-free survival (96.7% vs 54.1%) compared to definitive CRT alone

accessibility.rationale: Standard chemotherapy and radiation widely available; avoids major surgery which may appeal to patients; cisplatin 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks or 40 mg/m2 weekly are both accepted regimens

safety_profile.rationale: Pan-mucosal irradiation without surgery means higher total radiation burden to mucosal surfaces; severe mucositis, prolonged dysphagia, and significant weight loss common; 53% require hospitalization for symptom control during treatment

biomarker_match.rationale: Appropriate for HPV-negative CUP; comprehensive mucosal fields are specifically recommended for HPV-negative patients where primary site remains unidentified; PD-L1 CPS 5 does not influence CRT decision

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-dose cisplatin (100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks or 40 mg/m2 weekly) concurrent with definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) delivering 70 Gy to involved nodes and 54-60 Gy to bilateral pharyngeal, laryngeal, and hypopharyngeal mucosa plus bilateral cervical nodes. Cisplatin creates DNA adducts that inhibit repair of radiation-induced damage, enhancing tumor cell kill

key_evidence.study_name: Radiation Therapy in Management of HNCUP — Addition of Concurrent Chemotherapy
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics

notable_side_effects: Feeding tube dependence during and after treatment

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab Followed by Surgery and Adjuvant Pembrolizumab with Radiation (KEYNOTE-689 Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (KEYNOTE-689) in locally advanced HNSCC presented at AACR 2025 and published in NEJM; FDA-approved June 2025 for resectable LA-HNSCC with PD-L1 CPS >=1; paradigm-shifting trial

survival_benefit.rationale: Median EFS 59.7 vs 29.6 months for CPS >=1 (HR 0.70); major pathologic response rate 9.4% overall, 13.7% in CPS >=10; represents first approved neoadjuvant immunotherapy in HNSCC

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved but indication is for resectable locally advanced HNSCC; application to unknown primary requires clinical judgment and may be considered off-label; available at major cancer centers

safety_profile.rationale: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab generally well tolerated with no significant increase in surgical complications; adjuvant pembrolizumab + RT adds immune-related AEs; 3-4% risk of immune-mediated surgical delay

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 CPS 5 meets CPS >=1 threshold; benefit shown in CPS >=1 and >=10 subgroups; TP53 mutation may reduce pathologic complete response rates but does not preclude benefit

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab primes the immune system against tumor antigens before surgical resection; the presence of the intact tumor during checkpoint blockade maximizes T-cell activation. Adjuvant pembrolizumab with radiation maintains immune surveillance against residual micrometastatic disease while radiation provides locoregional control
notable_side_effects: Potential surgical delay from immune AEs (3-4%)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (June 2025) — resectable LA-HNSCC with CPS >=1

treatments.name: TPF Induction Chemotherapy (Docetaxel/Cisplatin/5-FU) Followed by Chemoradiation

evidence_level.rationale: TAX 324 and TAX 323 Phase 3 RCTs demonstrated superiority of TPF over PF induction; median OS 71 vs 30 months in TAX 323; established induction regimen for bulky or borderline resectable HNSCC

survival_benefit.rationale: TPF induction: median OS 18.8 months vs 14.5 for PF (HR 0.73, p=0.02); 3-year OS 62% vs 48%; pathologic CR rate 89%; may downstage N2b disease enabling better surgical outcomes

accessibility.rationale: All three drugs (docetaxel, cisplatin, 5-FU) FDA-approved and widely available; regimen requires inpatient infusion capabilities; can be administered at community oncology centers

safety_profile.rationale: Significant toxicity: grade 3/4 neutropenia common (requiring G-CSF support), febrile neutropenia risk, mucositis, renal toxicity from cisplatin, 5-FU cardiotoxicity; sequential approach means prolonged total treatment duration

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent; however TP53 mutation associated with cisplatin resistance (93% of high-risk TP53 patients show decreased sensitivity); 25 pack-year smoking history further associated with TP53-driven disease

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Docetaxel stabilizes microtubules preventing cell division; cisplatin creates DNA cross-links; 5-FU inhibits thymidylate synthase disrupting DNA synthesis. Triple combination provides maximal cytoreduction prior to definitive chemoradiation. Goal is to reduce tumor burden, treat micrometastatic disease, and improve locoregional control
biomarker_requirements: None specific; adequate renal function required for cisplatin

treatments.name: Cetuximab plus Radiation Therapy (for Cisplatin-Ineligible Patients)

evidence_level.rationale: Bonner et al. Phase 3 RCT (NEJM 2006) demonstrated OS benefit of cetuximab + RT vs RT alone (49 vs 29.3 months); NRG-HN004 compared cetuximab + RT to durvalumab + RT in cisplatin-ineligible patients

survival_benefit.rationale: Cetuximab + RT: median OS 49 months vs 29.3 months (HR 0.74); however inferior to cisplatin-based CRT in head-to-head comparisons; De-ESCALaTE showed no benefit over cisplatin; primarily reserved for cisplatin-ineligible

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved regimen; cetuximab widely available; appropriate alternative for patients with renal insufficiency, hearing loss, or other cisplatin contraindications

safety_profile.rationale: Better renal and ototoxicity profile than cisplatin; main toxicities are acneiform rash (grade 3/4 in 17%), radiation dermatitis, mucositis; infusion reactions (3%); overall better tolerated than CRT

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR is universally expressed in HNSCC but cetuximab response does not correlate with EGFR expression level; TP53 mutation may impair cetuximab efficacy; HPV-negative status does not preclude benefit

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cetuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting EGFR, blocking ligand binding and downstream RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling. It also mediates antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC). Combined with radiation, cetuximab inhibits radiation-induced EGFR activation and DNA damage repair, enhancing radiosensitivity

key_evidence.study_name: Bonner et al. — Cetuximab + RT vs RT alone

biomarker_requirements: None required; cisplatin ineligibility is the primary indication

treatments.name: Neck Dissection with Ipsilateral/Focused Radiation Therapy (De-escalated Approach)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple retrospective multicenter studies and propensity-score analyses comparing ipsilateral/focused RT to comprehensive mucosal RT; French multicenter study of 138 patients showed no OS difference; 2025 national retrospective cohort study confirmed equivalent outcomes

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 56-58% regardless of RT field extent; 5-year LRC 75-80%; primary emergence rate only 0.8-2.7%; however HPV-negative status specifically warrants comprehensive fields per ASCO guideline — de-escalation primarily validated for HPV-positive CUP

accessibility.rationale: Standard surgical and RT approach available at all centers; reduced RT fields may improve access at community centers with less advanced IMRT capabilities

safety_profile.rationale: Significantly lower toxicity than comprehensive mucosal irradiation: reduced weight loss, lower feeding tube rates, fewer opioid requirements, fewer unplanned hospitalizations; better quality of life outcomes

biomarker_match.rationale: CRITICAL LIMITATION: ASCO guidelines recommend comprehensive mucosal irradiation for HPV-negative CUP specifically because occult primary is more likely in pharyngeal/laryngeal sites; de-escalation is primarily validated for HPV-positive patients where base of tongue/tonsil primary is assumed; less appropriate for this HPV-negative patient

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neck dissection provides surgical clearance of involved lymph nodes with pathologic staging; focused/ipsilateral radiation therapy treats the surgical bed and ipsilateral neck without comprehensive pharyngeal/laryngeal mucosal coverage. The rationale is that with modern PET-CT and narrow-band endoscopy, the risk of occult mucosal primary emergence is low even without mucosal irradiation

key_evidence.study_name: National Retrospective Multicenter Cohort Study — Involved Neck Only vs Mucosal RT for HNSCCUP
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics

biomarker_requirements: Most data supports de-escalation in HPV-positive CUP
HPV-negative patients (like this one) are generally recommended comprehensive fields
notable_side_effects: Surgical complications (wound infection, nerve injury, lymphedema)
treatments.availability: Available — but ASCO guideline favors comprehensive mucosal irradiation for HPV-negative CUP

treatments.name: CRB-701 (Nectin-4 Targeting ADC) — for Platinum/IO-Refractory Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1/2 clinical trial with FDA Fast Track designation (2025); emerging ORR of 57% and DCR of 86% in R/M HNSCC patients who failed platinum + anti-PD-1; highly promising early data

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 57% and DCR 86% in heavily pretreated HNSCC represent meaningful responses in a refractory population; survival data still maturing; potential practice-changing if confirmed in Phase 3

accessibility.rationale: Currently available only through clinical trial enrollment; FDA Fast Track designation may accelerate approval timeline; limited trial sites

safety_profile.rationale: ADC-class toxicities expected: peripheral neuropathy, neutropenia, skin reactions; specific safety data still being collected; next-generation linker-payload may improve therapeutic index

biomarker_match.rationale: Nectin-4 is broadly expressed in squamous cell carcinomas; does not require specific PD-L1 or HPV status; TP53 mutation does not affect Nectin-4 expression; suitable for HPV-negative SCC

treatments.mechanism_of_action: CRB-701 (SYS6002) is a next-generation antibody-drug conjugate targeting Nectin-4, a cell adhesion molecule overexpressed in squamous cell carcinomas. The ADC delivers a cytotoxic payload directly to Nectin-4-expressing tumor cells, causing cell death while sparing normal tissue. The next-generation linker-payload design aims to improve therapeutic window
biomarker_requirements: Prior platinum and anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy required
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial — FDA Fast Track Designation

treatments.name: Tisotumab Vedotin (Tissue Factor-Targeting ADC)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 InnovaTV 207 trial in R/M HNSCC showed ORR of 32.5%; tissue factor is broadly expressed across squamous malignancies; further trials ongoing

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 32.5% in pretreated R/M HNSCC; durable responses observed; survival data maturing; represents meaningful activity in refractory setting

accessibility.rationale: Available through clinical trials; FDA-approved for cervical cancer (different indication); HNSCC indication still under investigation

safety_profile.rationale: Notable ocular toxicity (conjunctivitis, corneal changes) requiring prophylactic eye care; peripheral neuropathy; bleeding events; manageable with monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: Tissue factor broadly expressed in HNSCC regardless of HPV status or PD-L1 expression; not dependent on TP53 status; applicable to HPV-negative SCC

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tisotumab vedotin is a first-in-class ADC comprising a tissue factor (TF)-targeting antibody conjugated to the microtubule-disrupting agent MMAE via a cleavable linker. TF is highly expressed on HNSCC cells. Upon binding TF, the ADC is internalized and releases MMAE, causing mitotic arrest and cell death. Additional mechanisms include ADCC and bystander killing of adjacent tumor cells
biomarker_requirements: Tissue factor expression (broadly present in HNSCC)
notable_side_effects: Ocular toxicity (conjunctivitis, dry eye, corneal changes)

treatments.name: MRG003 (Becotatug Vedotin — Anti-EGFR ADC)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2a data showing ORR up to 55.2% and DCR 86.2% in nasopharyngeal carcinoma; EGFR-targeted ADC approach applicable to HNSCC; early-phase development

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 55.2% and DCR 86.2% at 2.3 mg/kg dose in platinum/PD-1-pretreated patients; impressive response rates but survival data immature; HNSCC-specific data still accumulating

accessibility.rationale: Currently in Phase 2 clinical trials primarily in Asia; limited global access; regulatory approval several years away

safety_profile.rationale: EGFR-ADC class: skin toxicity, interstitial lung disease risk, neutropenia; MMAE payload toxicities (neuropathy); manageable safety profile in early data

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR is almost universally expressed (>90%) in HNSCC; does not require specific PD-L1 status; HPV-negative HNSCC often has higher EGFR expression; TP53 mutation does not affect EGFR targeting

treatments.mechanism_of_action: MRG003 (becotatug vedotin) is a humanized anti-EGFR IgG1 antibody conjugated to MMAE. It targets EGFR-expressing tumor cells, is internalized upon receptor binding, and releases MMAE in the cytoplasm to disrupt microtubule assembly, causing mitotic arrest and apoptosis. Unlike cetuximab, the ADC approach delivers direct cytotoxic payload rather than relying solely on receptor blockade

key_evidence.study_name: MRG003 Phase 2a in R/M Head and Neck Cancer

treatments.name: AZD-1775 (Adavosertib — Wee-1 Kinase Inhibitor) for TP53-Mutant HNSCC

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1/2 clinical data in TP53-mutant solid tumors including HNSCC; synthetic lethality approach targeting the G2/M checkpoint in p53-deficient tumors; preclinical evidence of overcoming cisplatin resistance

survival_benefit.rationale: Early-phase data showing clinical activity in TP53-mutant tumors; potential to overcome cisplatin resistance which is highly relevant for this TP53-mutant patient; survival data limited

accessibility.rationale: Available through clinical trials only; AstraZeneca compound; limited enrollment sites globally

safety_profile.rationale: Dose-limiting toxicities include myelosuppression, diarrhea, and fatigue; narrow therapeutic window; combination with cisplatin may increase toxicity

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets the vulnerability created by TP53 mutation — this patient has confirmed TP53 mutation; synthetic lethality approach: TP53-mutant cells rely on Wee-1 for G2/M checkpoint, making them selectively sensitive to Wee-1 inhibition

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Adavosertib inhibits Wee-1 kinase, which regulates the G2/M cell cycle checkpoint. In TP53-mutant tumors (which lack the G1/S checkpoint due to p53 loss), Wee-1 inhibition forces cells through mitosis with unrepaired DNA damage, leading to mitotic catastrophe and cell death. This synthetic lethality approach selectively targets TP53-mutant cells while sparing normal p53-intact cells. When combined with cisplatin, it prevents repair of cisplatin-induced DNA damage

key_evidence.study_name: AZD-1775 Phase 1b in TP53-Mutant Solid Tumors

biomarker_requirements: TP53 mutation (this patient — confirmed TP53 mutant)

treatments.name: Micvotabart Pelidotin (MICVO — Anti-EDB Fibronectin ADC)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 preliminary data in R/M HNSCC; first-in-concept tumor microenvironment-targeting ADC; updated data expected mid-2026; Pyxis Oncology announced positive preliminary data December 2025

survival_benefit.rationale: Preliminary Phase 1 data showing clinical activity; specific ORR and survival data pending mid-2026 data presentation; novel mechanism targeting tumor ECM rather than tumor cells directly

accessibility.rationale: Phase 1 clinical trial only; very limited enrollment sites; several years from potential FDA approval

safety_profile.rationale: ECM-targeting approach may have different toxicity profile than cell-surface-targeting ADCs; preliminary safety data described as acceptable; combination with pembrolizumab also being studied

biomarker_match.rationale: EDB+ fibronectin is a component of tumor extracellular matrix broadly present in solid tumors; expression independent of HPV status, PD-L1, and TP53; universally applicable targeting strategy

treatments.mechanism_of_action: MICVO targets extradomain-B of fibronectin (EDB+FN), a non-cellular structural component of the tumor extracellular matrix (ECM) that is highly expressed in the tumor microenvironment but largely absent from normal tissues. By targeting the ECM rather than tumor cell surface antigens, MICVO delivers its cytotoxic payload to the tumor stroma, enabling bystander killing of surrounding tumor cells regardless of their surface antigen expression

key_evidence.study_name: MICVO Phase 1 Monotherapy in 2L+ R/M HNSCC

biomarker_requirements: EDB+ fibronectin expression (broadly expressed in solid tumors)

treatments.name: APR-246 (Eprenetapopt — Mutant p53 Reactivator) with Cisplatin

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1b/2 clinical trials in TP53-mutant solid tumors; PRIMA-1met/APR-246 has demonstrated ability to restore wild-type p53 conformation and function in mutant p53 proteins; primarily advanced in hematologic malignancies

survival_benefit.rationale: Limited clinical data in HNSCC specifically; preclinical studies show synergy with cisplatin by restoring p53-dependent apoptosis; early-phase solid tumor data showing modest activity

accessibility.rationale: Clinical trial access only; limited enrollment; primarily studied in MDS/AML; HNSCC trials limited

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well tolerated in clinical trials; main side effects are fatigue, dizziness, and headache; favorable safety profile for combination approaches

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets mutant p53 protein — this patient has confirmed TP53 mutation; mechanism specifically designed to restore wild-type p53 function in tumors with TP53 mutations; highly personalized approach

treatments.mechanism_of_action: APR-246 (eprenetapopt) is converted to methylene quinuclidinone (MQ) which binds covalently to cysteine residues in the core domain of mutant p53 protein, restoring its wild-type conformation and transcriptional activity. This reactivates p53-dependent apoptosis, cell cycle arrest, and senescence in tumor cells. When combined with cisplatin, restored p53 function enhances cisplatin-induced DNA damage response and apoptosis

key_evidence.study_name: APR-246 Phase 1b/2 in TP53-Mutant Solid Tumors

biomarker_requirements: TP53 mutation (this patient — confirmed TP53 mutant)
Specific TP53 mutation types may predict response

treatments.name: Intratumoral T4 CAR-T Cell Therapy (Anti-HER/pan-ErbB)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 trial in the United Kingdom investigating safety and feasibility of intratumoral CAR-T for HNSCC; first-in-human data; very early-stage development

survival_benefit.rationale: Safety and feasibility trial without efficacy data; CAR-T has shown transformative results in hematologic malignancies but solid tumor application remains challenging; theoretical potential for durable responses

accessibility.rationale: Phase 1 trial at limited UK sites only; autologous T-cell manufacturing required; extremely limited access; years from potential approval

safety_profile.rationale: Intratumoral delivery may reduce systemic CRS risk compared to IV CAR-T; on-target off-tumor toxicity to normal epithelial tissue expressing ErbB family members is a concern; limited safety data

biomarker_match.rationale: Pan-ErbB targeting covers HER1-4 receptors broadly expressed in HNSCC; independent of HPV status, PD-L1, and TP53; applicable to HPV-negative SCC

treatments.mechanism_of_action: T4 CAR-T therapy uses autologous T cells engineered with a chimeric antigen receptor targeting pan-ErbB (HER) receptors. Intratumoral injection delivers CAR-T cells directly into the tumor microenvironment, bypassing trafficking barriers that limit IV CAR-T efficacy in solid tumors. The T4 CAR recognizes multiple HER family members, reducing antigen escape risk

key_evidence.study_name: T4 Intratumoral CAR-T Phase 1 in HNSCC

biomarker_requirements: ErbB/HER family expression (broadly expressed in HNSCC)
Not candidates for conventional active treatment
notable_side_effects: Potential cytokine release syndrome (likely reduced with intratumoral delivery)

clinical_trials.title: Chemoradiation vs Immunotherapy and Radiation for Locally Advanced Head and Neck Cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: Comparing standard cisplatin-CRT to immunotherapy-RT combinations; aims to determine if checkpoint inhibitors can replace cisplatin as radiosensitizer with potentially better toxicity profile

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant and Adjuvant Pembrolizumab in Resectable Locally Advanced Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Median EFS 59.7 vs 29.6 months (HR 0.70) for CPS >=1; major pathologic response 9.4% overall, 13.7% in CPS >=10; FDA approval for neoadjuvant/adjuvant pembrolizumab with radiotherapy

clinical_trials.title: CRB-701 (Nectin-4 ADC) in Pretreated Recurrent or Metastatic HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Emerging ORR 57%, DCR 86% in 37 evaluable HNSCC patients; next-generation Nectin-4 targeting ADC with improved linker-payload

clinical_trials.title: Micvotabart Pelidotin (MICVO) Monotherapy and Combination with Pembrolizumab in 2L+ R/M HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Positive preliminary Phase 1 data announced December 2025; first-in-concept ECM-targeting ADC; updated data expected mid-2026; combination with pembrolizumab arm also enrolling

clinical_trials.title: Radiotherapy with Cetuximab or Durvalumab for Locoregionally Advanced HNSCC — Cisplatin Ineligible
clinical_trials.early_results: Compared cetuximab-RT to durvalumab-RT in cisplatin-ineligible patients; results published in Lancet Oncology 2024; establishes options for patients who cannot receive cisplatin

clinical_trials.title: Intratumoral T4 CAR-T Cell Therapy for Recurrent/Refractory HNSCC
biomarker_requirements: Not candidates for conventional active treatment
clinical_trials.early_results: First-in-human study investigating safety and feasibility of intratumoral CAR-T delivery; novel approach to overcome solid tumor CAR-T barriers

combination_strategies.rationale: KEYNOTE-689 demonstrated that adding neoadjuvant pembrolizumab before surgery followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab with radiation significantly improves EFS (HR 0.70). For this HPV-negative patient with PD-L1 CPS 5 (meets CPS >=1 threshold), perioperative immunotherapy addresses both local and systemic micrometastatic disease. Neoadjuvant checkpoint blockade primes antitumor immunity while the intact tumor mass is present, maximizing T-cell activation and enabling pathologic response assessment

For patients who progress on first-line KEYNOTE-048 regimen, sequential CRB-701 targets a different pathway (Nectin-4 cell adhesion) independent of PD-L1 and EGFR. The ADC mechanism bypasses immunotherapy resistance mechanisms. ORR of 57% in platinum/IO-pretreated HNSCC suggests non-cross-resistant activity. TP53 mutation does not affect Nectin-4 expression, maintaining target validity

This patient's TP53 mutation creates vulnerability to Wee-1 inhibition via synthetic lethality. TP53-mutant cells are 10-fold more likely to show cisplatin resistance, but AZD-1775 forces cells through mitosis with unrepaired DNA damage, overcoming this resistance. Combining Wee-1 inhibition with cisplatin-CRT could restore chemosensitivity in this TP53-mutant, HPV-negative SCC where cisplatin efficacy may be compromised

For bulky N2b disease, TPF induction can achieve 89% pathologic CR rate, potentially downstaging the neck before definitive surgery. Sequential approach: TPF induction to reduce nodal burden, followed by neck dissection for pathologic assessment, then adjuvant CRT to comprehensive mucosal fields. The 25 pack-year smoking history increases concern for occult second primaries, supporting aggressive multimodal approach

MICVO targets the tumor extracellular matrix (ECM) rather than tumor cells, potentially synergizing with checkpoint blockade by disrupting the immunosuppressive stromal barrier. ECM-targeting may enhance T-cell infiltration while ADC payload kills surrounding tumor cells. Phase 1/2 combination arm actively enrolling; particularly relevant for patients progressing on single-agent immunotherapy

supportive_care.approach: Nutritional Prehabilitation and Ongoing Dietitian Support
supportive_care.evidence: Systematic reviews demonstrate that early nutritional counseling with oral nutritional supplements preserves body weight and serum albumin during CRT, reduces severity of oral mucositis, and decreases hospital stay. Sarcopenia is common in head and neck cancer patients and worsens treatment outcomes.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintenance of body weight (10-15% weight loss is typical without intervention); reduced treatment interruptions; improved cisplatin tolerance; preserved swallowing function; lower PEG tube dependence. Nutritional prehabilitation before CRT improves treatment completion rates

supportive_care.evidence: Continued smoking during and after treatment for HNSCC significantly increases risk of second primary cancers, treatment complications, radiation mucositis severity, and overall mortality. This patient's 25 pack-year history places him at high risk. Combination pharmacotherapy (nicotine replacement + bupropion or varenicline) with behavioral counseling achieves highest quit rates.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced risk of second primary cancers (critical concern in HPV-negative SCC with TP53 mutation); improved radiation treatment response; reduced mucositis severity; improved wound healing after neck dissection; improved long-term survival

supportive_care.evidence: Comprehensive mucosal irradiation in HPV-negative CUP causes severe mucositis in 40-50% of patients. Evidence-based management includes: oral cryotherapy during cisplatin infusion, low-level laser therapy (photobiomodulation), benzydamine mouthwash, and systematic pain management. MASCC/ISOO guidelines provide evidence-based protocols.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced severity and duration of oral mucositis; decreased opioid requirements; fewer unplanned hospitalizations (53% of CRT patients require admission for symptom control); improved ability to maintain oral intake; fewer treatment interruptions

supportive_care.approach: Structured Exercise Program During and After Treatment
supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses show that structured exercise programs during and after head and neck cancer treatment improve quality of life, reduce fatigue, maintain functional capacity, and may improve survival outcomes. Both aerobic and resistance training components are beneficial. Start prehabilitation before treatment when possible.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced cancer-related fatigue; improved physical function and quality of life; maintenance of lean body mass during treatment; potential survival benefit; improved mental health and reduced depression

supportive_care.approach: Speech and Swallowing Therapy (Preventive and Rehabilitative)
supportive_care.evidence: Comprehensive mucosal irradiation to bilateral pharynx and larynx causes acute and chronic dysphagia. Preventive swallowing exercises started before radiation reduce long-term dysphagia severity and aspiration risk. The Mendelsohn maneuver, effortful swallow, and tongue-base exercises preserve swallowing musculature.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced long-term dysphagia severity; lower aspiration pneumonia risk; improved oral intake and nutrition; potential for PEG tube avoidance or earlier removal; better quality of life

supportive_care.evidence: Head and neck cancer patients experience among the highest rates of psychological distress, depression, and anxiety of all cancer types. HPV-negative diagnosis with worse prognosis compounds psychological burden. Regular distress screening (NCCN Distress Thermometer) with referral to psycho-oncology when indicated improves treatment adherence and quality of life.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved treatment adherence; reduced depression and anxiety; better coping with treatment side effects and body image changes; improved overall quality of life; potential survival benefit through improved treatment completion

sources.title: Diagnosis and Management of Squamous Cell Carcinoma of Unknown Primary in the Head and Neck: ASCO Guideline
Factors Influencing the Outcome of Head and Neck Cancer of Unknown Primary (HNCUP)
